text: translation of the book (the same translation used in all later editions by Routledge and Chicago) was done by Monika B. Wizedom and Gabrielle L. Caffee, supervised by Kimball himself. It is a good translation. In its passage to the Anglo-Saxon world the book got itself a new subtitle. The French version had been published by É. Nourry 2 (Paris) as Les Rites de passage, étude systématique des rites de la porte et du seuil, de l’hospitalité, de l’adoption, de la grossesse et de l’accouchement, de la naissance, de l’enfance, de la puberté, de l’initiation, de l’ordination, du couronnement, des fiançailles et du mariage, des funérailles, des saisons, etc. Books published in that period often had extremely long and explanatory subtitles, sometimes close to complete outlines of the actual content, and quite typically ending with an ‘etc.’. Not surprisingly, in French- speaking environments, the book has most often been abbreviated as Les Rites de passage, étude systématique, or simply Rites de Passage. The University of Chicago Press together with Kimball chose to give the book the title we are now familiar with: The Rites of Passage. A classic study of cultural 2 Nourry was van Gennep’s friend, who himself wrote about folklore under a pseudonym (De Saintyves). Nourry had helped van Gennep in the preparation of the book. Van Gennep therefore ends his foreword with these cryptic words: ‘Thus the publisher has been the victim of the scholar and friend within him. I hope, at least, that he will not be a victim of the reader’ (1960: XXVI). Liminality and the Modern 74 celebrations. There might have been some discussion on how to translate ‘rites de passage’. An alternative could have been ‘ritual passages’, which is probably the English term that most people would use today. But ‘rites of passage’ stays closer to the French original. The subtitle was altogether new. In a way it remains a dubious subtitle: ‘cultural celebrations’ is a rather vague term, and actually not one used by Van Gennep, who rather spoke of ‘ceremonies’ – so this would have been a more correct word. 3 On the other hand, the publishers were surely right on target about the term ‘classic’, although they could not possibly have known that with any certainty in 1960. The book had not even remotely established as a ‘classic’ within French anthropology. It had, after all, been written more than 50 years earlier, by an author nobody really knew, and the book had made almost no impact. But Kimball was sure that this would change with the new publication in English. And here he was right. A forgotten book did become a classic with half a century of delay. So far so good. Rites of Passage was reprinted in 1965. The later editions, from 1977, 1980, 2004 and 2010 are hardly worth mentioning, except for the fact that these are the editions that most students use today. The Routledge paperback version of The Rites of Passage that appeared in 1977 surprisingly dropped Kimball’s Introduction, although it still figured on the title page and in the table of contents. Amazingly enough, this error has not been rectified since: in all later editions, Kimball’s introduction still figures in the table of contents, but it is not there for anyone to read. Moreover, and more seriously, no alternative introduction was written. All the reader gets as context is Arnold van Gennep’s own foreword finished in December 1908, with implicit references that no contemporary reader will be able to decipher. It is not much of a help for new readers encountering van Gennep with a century of delay. On the one hand, one could argue that a thorough introduction is no longer necessary, as the book itself has by now become famous. On the other hand, the lack of a proper introduction to such an important book must be seen as yet another symptom of the continuous neglect of Arnold van Gennep’s work. The perception that this book ‘speaks for itself’ is deeply erroneous. If any, this is a book that requires a proper framing. More nuanced introductions to the book have been written for other translations (see for example the Introduction by Remotti written for the 1981 Italian translation). The book stands in desperate need of a new introduction and proper contextualization in the English translation. As it is, the Introduction to the book written by Kimball is the only one ever written in English; next to shorter Encyclopaedia entries, it is probably also the most read introduction to Arnold van Gennep’s work as such. This, it must be said, is rather unfortunate. In his Introduction, Kimball identified van Gennep as a member of a generation of French sociologists inspired by positivism, and suggested that van Gennep be seen as an example of French scholars like Mauss, 3 In the 1969 French re-edition, which included van Gennep’s own notes to the first publication, van Gennep himself indicates that it might have been better to replace ‘rites’ with ‘ceremonies’. Liminality Rediscovered: With Victor Turner and Beyond 75 Hubert and Durkheim who were collectively developing a functionalist approach (Kimball 1960: vii). This contextualization of van Gennep’s work is both right and wrong and for reasons already explained: van Gennep was indeed involved in developing an entire ‘approach’, but it was not really ‘functionalist’ in the sense that readers in 1960 would understand the term, and it happened in a struggle against the Durkheimians, not simply as a ‘member’ of that Durkheimian generation. Kimball only slowly started to find out about this struggle after 1960, as it had been hidden away from sight, even within France. Therefore, while the publication of the book in English made van Gennep a ‘new classic’ with half a century of delay, the Introduction certainly did not facilitate a proper reception, for it bypassed how and why van Gennep had been ostracized by the academic world, and how his theoretical project differed from that of Durkheim and his followers.
